context: In Galatians chapter number four in verses five and six, and what great truth that is given to us with regard to this relationship
point_summary: Adoption into God's family—being made His child through Christ's redemptive work—is presented as a foundational truth that even the youngest believer begins to grasp.

context: but when the fullness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son made of a woman, made under the law
point_summary: God's sending of his Son in the fullness of time accomplished redemption and adoption, so that believers can cry "Abba, Father" — establishing the Father-child relationship that underlies all fellowship with God.

context: To redeem them that were under the law
point_summary: Redemption through Christ's blood accomplishes more than forgiveness—it secures the adoption of sons, establishing the Father-child relationship that makes fellowship with God possible.

context: and because ye are sons, God hath sent forth the spirit of his Son into your hearts crying, Abba, Father
point_summary: Galatians 4:6 grounds the father-child relationship in the indwelling Spirit, who enables the believer to cry "Abba, Father"—the intimate basis on which all fellowship with God rests.

context: walking in the spirit and not fulfilling the lust of the flesh
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Galatians 2:20's language of being "crucified with Christ" is cited as the posture of surrender that enabled Paul to fulfill his God-appointed mission of suffering witness before Gentiles, kings, and Israel.

context: Notice in the book of Galatians in chapter number five
point_summary: Paul's suffering persecution is presented as proof that he preached the offense of the cross rather than circumcision, since compromising that message would have ended the opposition against him.

context: five in verse number 11, and I brethren, if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution?
point_summary: Paul's suffering persecution rather than preaching circumcision proves that he stood for grace alone in salvation, not grace plus works, and that the cross itself was the offense drawing opposition.

context: if you read through the book of Galatians in the early chapters, you find exactly that Paul is referencing that, that council in Jerusalem
point_summary: Paul's refusal to preach circumcision as necessary for salvation—grace plus nothing, Christ alone—is identified as the very reason he suffered persecution from the Judaizers troubling both Galatia and Corinth.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Crucifixion with Christ, drawn from Galatians 2:20, grounds Paul's declaration that his life is not his own—he belongs entirely to God and exists only to finish his appointed course.

context: we're to be crucified with Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if I'm crucified with Christ
point_summary: Living crucified with Christ—freed from self-concern—enables bold, effective gospel witness regardless of others' rejection or ridicule.

context: Paul said this in Galatians 1 and verse 10, for do I now persuade men or God? Or do I seek to please men? For if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ.
point_summary: Seeking God's approval rather than human approval is the governing ambition of authentic ministry, because pleasing men and being Christ's servant are mutually exclusive pursuits.

context: I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel
point_summary: Paul's use of "another gospel" in Galatians 1:6 does not imply a legitimate alternative; what false teachers preach is corrupt, not a true equal to the genuine gospel of grace.

context: it's not another, but there be some that trouble you and would pervert the gospel of Christ
point_summary: Any so-called "other gospel" is not a legitimate alternative but a corruption of the true gospel, making it damnable rather than merely different.

context: but though we or an angel from heaven preach any other gospel unto you than that which we have preached unto you, let him be accursed
point_summary: Even an angel preaching a gospel different from what Paul delivered stands under God's curse—anathema—because the alternative is not a parallel gospel but a corrupt one.

context: As we said before, so say I now again, if any man preach any other gospel unto you than that you have received, let him be accursed
point_summary: Any gospel differing from what was originally preached stands under divine curse—anathema—a severity Paul invokes again in Galatians 1:9 to underscore that no corrupted substitute gospel carries equal standing with the true one.

context: I received it not of man, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ
point_summary: Paul's apostolic authority rests not on human tradition but on direct revelation from Jesus Christ, validating both his message and his integrity against those who attacked the man behind the ministry.

context: let him that is taught in the word communicate unto him that teacheth in all good things
point_summary: Galatians 6:6 is cited as Paul's parallel teaching that those receiving spiritual instruction are obligated to materially support their teacher, establishing the legitimacy of ministerial compensation.

context: the book of Galatians and chapter number one, Galatians chapter number one
point_summary: Paul's letter to the Galatian churches, established during the first missionary journey of Acts 13–14, is introduced as the textual backdrop for examining personal transformation under the power of an omnipotent God.

context: they were so soon removed in verse number six from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel
point_summary: Paul's astonishment that the Galatian churches so quickly abandoned the grace of Christ for a perverted gospel frames the letter's urgent concern with false teachers who mix grace and works for salvation.

context: which is not another, but there be some that trouble you and would pervert the gospel of Christ
point_summary: False teachers who mix grace and works do not offer a different gospel but pervert the one true gospel—a distortion Paul identifies as troubling the Galatian churches.

context: but I certify you, brethren, that the gospel which was preached of me is not after man
point_summary: Paul's authority to declare the gospel rests not on human tradition or instruction but on direct revelation from Jesus Christ, making any addition of works to grace a perversion of that divinely given message.

context: for ye have heard of my conversation in time past in the Jews' religion, how that beyond measure I persecuted the church of
point_summary: Paul's former life of extreme persecution of the church and zeal for Jewish tradition establishes the dramatic before-picture that makes his transformation by divine revelation all the more striking.

context: but, in verse number 15
point_summary: God's sovereign call—separating Paul from birth and calling him by grace—marks the decisive "but" that divides what Paul was from what he became, apart from any human agency.

context: but when it pleased God who separated me from my mother's womb and called me by his grace to reveal his son in me
point_summary: God's sovereign call—separating Paul from birth and calling him by grace—marks the decisive "but" that divides what Paul was from what he became, apart from any human conferral or apostolic validation.

context: that I might preach him among the heathen, immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood
point_summary: God's sovereign call to Paul—separating him from birth, revealing His Son *in* him, and commissioning him to preach among the Gentiles—came by divine pleasure and grace, not human instruction or apostolic conferral.

context: Neither when I up to Jerusalem to them which were apostles before me, but I went into Arabia and returned again into Damascus
point_summary: Paul's retreat to Arabia rather than Jerusalem after his conversion demonstrates that his gospel came by divine revelation alone, not from human apostolic instruction.

context: Even after three years I went up to Jerusalem to see Peter and abode with him fifteen days
point_summary: Paul's three-year delay before visiting Jerusalem underscores that his gospel came by divine revelation alone, not from apostolic instruction or human authority.

context: But other of the apostles saw I none, save James the Lord's brother
point_summary: Paul's independent reception of the gospel is confirmed by his limited contact with the apostles—seeing only Peter and James during his first Jerusalem visit.

context: Now the things which I write unto you, behold, before God I lie not
point_summary: Paul's oath in verse 20 — "before God, I lie not" — anchors his entire account of receiving the gospel by direct revelation as a solemn, God-witnessed testimony, not human invention.

context: Afterwards I came into the regions of Syria and Cilicia and was unknown by face unto the
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but they had heard only that he which persecuted us in times past now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed
point_summary: Paul's radical transformation from persecutor to preacher, known even to churches who had never seen his face, demonstrates that grace produces a verifiable, public change of identity.

context: for you have heard of my conversation in time past
point_summary: Paul's former life of violent persecution of the church, described in verse 13 as his "conversation in time past," forms the before-side of a contrast completed in verse 23 by his transformed identity.

context: We noticed that in Galatians, right?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I said this to you in chapter one of Galatians
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: walk in the Spirit so that we do not fulfill the lust of the flesh
point_summary: Walking in the Spirit requires continual, repeated yielding because the flesh remains alive and actively reasserts control, preventing the Holy Spirit from having full possession of the believer.

context: the Apostle Paul dealt with that quite in depth in the book of Galatians
point_summary: Paul's argument in Galatians is cited as establishing that the law cannot save mankind but instead exposes human sinfulness and the need for a Savior.

context: in the book of Galatians he makes it abundantly clear
point_summary: The law, as Paul argues throughout Galatians, cannot save mankind but instead reveals human sinfulness and the need for a Savior.

context: look over in Galatians, if you will, just a few pages back
point_summary: Righteousness cannot come through law-keeping or human effort; if it could, Christ's death would have been purposeless and in vain.

context: I do not frustrate the grace of God. For if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain.
point_summary: If righteousness could come through law-keeping, Christ's death would have been purposeless — but his willing sacrifice proves that grace, not human effort, is the only path to redemption.

context: Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary bearing of the law's curse—becoming a curse in our place on the cross—is what redeems sinners from condemnation that the law could only pronounce but never remove.

context: cursed is everyone that hangeth on a tree
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary bearing of the law's curse — becoming a curse for us on the cross — is presented as the redemptive act that frees sinners condemned by a law that could never give life.

context: but when the fullness of the time was come, God sent forth his son made of a woman made under the law
point_summary: God's sending of his Son at the precise moment of "the fullness of the time" was not accidental but part of a redemptive plan devised before the foundation of the world, executed to redeem those under the law's condemnation.

context: In Galatians four, in verse number five
point_summary: Christ's redemptive work under the law was purposeful—to liberate those condemned by the law and secure for them the standing of adopted sons before God.

context: God sent forth his son. He was made of a woman made under the law.
point_summary: Galatians 4:4 is read to show that Christ's incarnation and subjection to the law were not accidental but unfolded according to a divine plan purposed before the foundation of the world.

context: to redeem them that were under the law that we might receive the adoption of sons
point_summary: Christ's redemption of those under the law was not accidental but executed at the precise moment of God's eternal plan, with the express purpose of securing adoptive sonship for believers.

context: because you're sons, God had sent forth the spirit of his son to your hearts crying, Abba, Father
point_summary: Redemption's purpose in Galatians 4:4–6 is shown to move from God's eternal plan, through Christ's substitutionary curse-bearing, to the Spirit-given cry of "Abba, Father" that marks adopted sons.

reference_text: Galatians (Abraham and the promise of the seed)
context: if you read the book of Galatians, when he talks about Abraham and the promise of the seed, and he did not say seeds plural, but seed as in singular, speaking of Christ
point_summary: Galatians' singular "seed" language identifies Christ specifically as the promised offspring of Abraham, explaining why the incarnation—not angelic intervention—was necessary to atone for sin.

context: you did run well, who hindered you that you should not obey the truth
point_summary: Even the genuinely saved can be derailed by false teaching, as Paul's rebuke in Galatians 5:7 warns believers who began well that something has hindered their obedience to the truth.

context: We find that as we study the book of Galatians, it's written to the churches in that region of Galatia
point_summary: The book of Galatians is identified as correspondence to the churches planted in the region of Galatia during Paul's first missionary journey, on which John Mark also traveled.

context: I do not frustrate the grace of God in Galatians 2 and verse 21, for if righteousness came by the law, then Christ is dead in vain
point_summary: Righteousness cannot come through human works or offerings — if it could, Christ's death would be meaningless — so God's only acceptable means of forgiveness is through the blood of Christ alone.

context: Be not deceived. God is not mocked. Whatsoever man soweth, that shall he also reap
point_summary: Galatians 6:7's law of sowing and reaping applies in both directions—sowing good seed yields good fruit, not only the negative consequences most readers assume.

context: if you look at the context of Galatians 6 it's positive and negative because if we sow good seed, we reap good fruit
point_summary: Galatians 6's law of sowing and reaping cuts both ways — sowing bad seed yields bad fruit, but sowing good seed yields good fruit, correcting a common negative-only reading of the passage.

context: if righteousness came by the law then Christ is dead in vain Galatians
point_summary: Embracing biblical truth about salvation—that righteousness cannot come by law or human effort, so Christ's death would be meaningless otherwise—is invoked as the same logic requiring rejection of cultural opinions about marriage.

context: What so ever man soweth, that shall he also reap
point_summary: Sowing and reaping is presented as an inviolable divine principle — both positive and negative — invoked to counter Satan's lie that sin's consequences will not come.

context: We understand that in Galatians 6
point_summary: The law of sowing and reaping in Galatians 6 is invoked as a divine principle—operating both positively and negatively—to counter Satan's lie that sin's consequences will not come.

context: as we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men, especially unto them who are of the household of faith
point_summary: Opportunity to do good is fleeting, demanding that believers seize each God-given moment to bless others before circumstances change and the opening passes.

context: as we have therefore opportunity
point_summary: Opportunity to do good is fleeting and must be seized in the present moment, because circumstances change, lives end, and the chance to bless others may not come again.

context: Let us do good unto all men, especially unto them who are of the household of faith. We see this in Galatians 6.10
point_summary: Opportunity to do good is urgent and finite — Galatians 6:10's call to act "as we have therefore opportunity" is pressed as a warning that circumstances change, people die, and Christ's return draws near.

context: the fruit of the Spirit is, don't say that you're walking in the Spirit, but there's no love, joy, peace, longsuffering
point_summary: Just as the fruit of the Spirit evidences genuine Spirit-walking, Spirit-filled singing—psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs—evidences genuine Spirit-filling, not merely a claimed experience.

context: when the fullness of time was come God sent forth his son born of a virgin made under the law
point_summary: The precision of Christ's first coming — arriving in "the fullness of time," not a moment early or late — grounds the confidence that his second coming will be equally exact and unstoppable.

context: if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain
point_summary: If righteousness could come by human effort, Christ's death would be meaningless — but because he died, his cross stands as proof that no one could earn redemption any other way.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am crucified with Christ" from Galatians 2:20 is cited as evidence that he had already made himself a living sacrifice, fully surrendered to God before urging others to do the same in Romans 12.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Crucifixion with Christ, drawn from Galatians 2:20, is the stated reason Paul could face bonds and afflictions unmoved — having already surrendered his life, nothing could threaten what he no longer counted as his own.

context: Notice what Paul says to the believers in Galatia, the churches in the region of Galatia are being written to, and in Galatians chapter number 5
point_summary: Satan actively seeks to hinder believers from finishing their course with joy, as Paul's question in Galatians 5:7 implies that outside interference, not personal failure, can derail a race that was running well.

context: Galatians 5 and verse number 7, the Bible says, ye did run well. Who did hinder you that you should not obey the truth?
point_summary: The Galatians' early faithfulness ("ye did run well") is held up as a warning that a good start means nothing if the enemy's hindrance prevents a believer from finishing the course with joy.

context: We find that in Galatians chapter four, not concerning this particular church, obviously,
point_summary: Paul's willingness to ask "Am I therefore become your enemy because I tell you the truth?" illustrates that faithful proclamation of God's word can provoke personal hostility even from those the preacher genuinely loves.

context: he which once persecuted now preaches
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for brethren, you have been called unto liberty, only use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh, but by love serve one another
point_summary: Liberty in Christ is not a license for self-indulgence; Galatians 5:13 is cited to show that Christian freedom is bounded by love, not exploited for fleshly ends.

context: It's our schoolmaster, according to the book of Galatians
point_summary: The law's role as a "schoolmaster" (Gal. 3:24) is invoked to show that Old Testament sacrificial ritual, including the Day of Atonement, pointed forward to Christ rather than providing salvation itself.

context: he did not receive this gospel from men but by the revelation of Jesus Christ
point_summary: Paul's direct reception of the gospel by revelation of Jesus Christ, not from men, is cited as evidence of his intimate, personal walk with God.

context: in the region of Galatia when you read the book of Galatians
point_summary: The letter to the Galatians is addressed to multiple churches across a region, all of which were planted during this first missionary journey recorded in Acts 13–14.

context: Be not deceived, God is not mocked. For whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap
point_summary: Self-deception is identified as the gravest danger the verse warns against, with the sowing-and-reaping principle presented as an inviolable divine law governing both spiritual and carnal choices.

context: there's your flesh and your spirit in enmity one with the other
point_summary: Knowing Scripture's divine origin and authority does not guarantee obedience, because the flesh and spirit remain in conflict, pulling believers away from the Word they know to be true.

context: In Galatians 5 and verse number nine, the Bible says a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump
point_summary: A little leaven leavening the whole lump illustrates how the doubt of ten spies spread to corrupt an entire congregation of millions, proving that unbelief never stays contained to the individual.

reference_text: a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump
context: a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump. Just a little impacts the whole
point_summary: A single person's doubt, like leaven spreading through dough, can corrupt and carry an entire congregation — as ten spies' unbelief swept millions into rebellion.

context: Galatians chapter number 3 and verse number 11
point_summary: The fourfold repetition of "the just shall live by faith" across Habakkuk, Romans, Galatians, and Hebrews signals divine emphasis that God's people are defined by a life of faith rather than sight.

context: Galatians chapter number five
point_summary: Joy is identified as part of the singular "fruit of the Spirit" in Galatians 5, not a plural collection, grounding the gladness of Psalm 4:7 in the Spirit's own work.

context: this I say then, walk in the spirit and ye shall not fulfill the lust of the flesh
point_summary: Walking in the Spirit (Gal. 5:16) is presented as the condition that produces the singular fruit of the Spirit—including joy—as evidence of genuine Spirit-filled living.

context: the Bible says the fruit of the spirit, notice in Galatians 5 in verse 22
point_summary: Joy is identified as part of the singular "fruit of the Spirit"—not plural fruits—serving as biblical evidence that those truly filled with the Holy Spirit receive this gladness from God.

context: the fruit of the spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance
point_summary: The singular "fruit of the spirit" — not fruits — encompasses love, joy, peace, and the rest as unified biblical evidence that a believer is genuinely filled with the Holy Spirit.

context: they that are Christ have crucified the flesh with the affections and lust
point_summary: Crucifying the flesh with its affections and lusts, as verse 24 describes, is the condition under which the Spirit produces genuine joy and gladness that human effort alone cannot replicate.

context: If we live in the spirit, let us also walk in the spirit
point_summary: Living in the Spirit, as Galatians 5:25 calls believers to do, produces the singular fruit of the Spirit—including joy—as observable evidence of genuine Spirit-filled life, distinct from any self-generated gladness.

context: Galatians chapter number five, walk in the spirit and you shall not fulfill the lust of the flesh
point_summary: Joy is forfeited, not stolen—believers lose it by choosing to walk in the flesh rather than the Spirit, since God commands but does not compel Spirit-filled living.

context: in the same text of scripture in Galatians chapter number five, when the Bible provides for us what the fruit of the spirit is
point_summary: Joy is a gift of the Spirit that believers can forfeit through choosing to walk in the flesh rather than the Spirit, since God commands but does not compel Spirit-filled living.

context: the fruit of the spirit
point_summary: Joy, listed among the fruit of the Spirit in Galatians 5:22, is presented here as evidence of Spirit-filled living that should be visibly recognizable to others.

context: That's why the Apostle Paul says in the book of Galatians, I am crucified with Christ.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am crucified with Christ" is cited alongside Galatians 5:16–17 to explain why believers, though saved, still battle the unfilled flesh rather than walking consistently in the Spirit.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Galatians 2:20 is cited as scriptural grounding for the ongoing internal conflict between the old nature and the new, explaining why believers struggle to remain filled with the Spirit rather than self.

context: We find that he tells us in Galatians chapter number five
point_summary: Galatians 5:16–17 is cited to establish that the believer's ongoing struggle with self-filling rather than Spirit-filling is rooted in the inherent, unresolved conflict between the old nature and the new nature.

context: This I say then, walk in the spirit and you shall not fulfill the lust of the flesh
point_summary: Walking in the Spirit is the prescribed remedy for the believer's internal conflict between the old and new natures, enabling them not to fulfill the lusts of the flesh.

context: For the flesh lusteth against the spirit and the spirit against the flesh. And these are contrary the one to the other
point_summary: Galatians 5:17 is cited to identify the internal conflict between the old and new natures as the very reason Spirit-filling is both necessary and contested for the believer.

context: but the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance. Against such there is no law.
point_summary: The fruit of the Spirit listed in Galatians 5:22–23 is presented as the biblical evidence that a person is filled with the Holy Ghost, demonstrating Christlikeness rather than mere self-generated boldness.

context: I am crucified with Christ
point_summary: Being filled with the Holy Ghost requires being emptied of self — a continuous reality captured in "I am crucified with Christ," which the sermon presents as the necessary response whenever self revives.

context: Notice in Galatians chapter 4, Paul had the opportunity to go on the first missionary
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: brethren, I beseech you, be as I am, for I am as ye are, ye have not injured me at all. Ye know how through infirmity of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first, and my temptation which was in the flesh, in my flesh ye despise not, nor reject it, but receive me, notice, as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus
point_summary: Paul's appeal in Galatians 4:12 illustrates the volatile nature of pastoral relationships, where a congregation that once received him as an angel of God could quickly turn against him.

context: you received me as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus, where is then the blessedness ye speak of? For I bear ye record, notice what he says, if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes and have given them to me
point_summary: Receiving a faithful minister "as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus" can swiftly reverse to treating him as an enemy the moment he speaks unwelcome truth.

context: am I, verse 16, therefore become your enemy because I tell you the truth
point_summary: Truth-telling can transform a beloved messenger into a perceived enemy, as Paul's experience with the Galatian churches illustrates—he moved from being received as an angel to being treated as an adversary solely because he spoke honestly.

context: in verse 17, they zealously affect you but not well
point_summary: False teachers, Paul warns, pursue the Galatians with zeal that is not genuinely for their good but to draw the Galatians' loyalty away from Paul toward themselves.

context: My little children of whom I travail in birth again until Christ be formed in you
point_summary: Paul's anguish over the Galatians—travailing in birth again until Christ be formed in them—illustrates how truth-telling can transform a beloved apostle into a perceived enemy in the eyes of those he loves.

context: I desire to be present with you now and to change my voice for I stand in doubt of you
point_summary: Paul's longing to be present with the Galatians and change his tone reflects the relational tension created when truth-telling turns a beloved messenger from angel to enemy in the eyes of those he loves.

context: when the fullness of time was come, that God did in fact send forth his son, and we look at that truth as Paul would mention in Galatians
point_summary: The virgin birth and incarnation of Christ, described in Matthew 1 and Galatians 4:4, are presented as the direct fulfillment of the proto-evangelium announced in Genesis 3:15.

context: but God forbid that I should glory save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ by whom the world is crucified unto me and I unto the world
point_summary: Because God transformed the gore of crucifixion into glory, Paul's boast in the cross alone (Gal. 6:14) becomes the natural response—and the model for a Calvary-compelled ministry.

context: I am crucified with Christ. He declares that in Galatians 2 in verse number 20
point_summary: Paul's total surrender to Christ — "I am crucified with Christ" — is cited as the defining posture that made him a faithful, will-of-God-following example worthy of imitation.

context: Galatians chapter two
point_summary: Paul's declaration in Galatians 2 is presented as the biographical evidence that he had genuinely fulfilled the three-step order of Mark 8:34—self-denial, cross-bearing, and then following Christ.

context: I am crucified with Christ. Nevertheless, I live, yet not I, but Christ liveth in me. In the life which I now live in the flesh, I live by the faith of the son of God who loved me and gave himself for me
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am crucified with Christ" is cited as evidence that he had genuinely fulfilled the prior conditions of self-denial and cross-bearing that make authentic Christ-following possible.
